exemplar.title: Are the principal models of urban structure reflected in the urban morphology of Basel?

detailedExemplarData.title: Are the principal models of urban structure reflected in the urban morphology of Basel?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear, geographically focused research question with explicit links to urban landâuse theory allowing hypothesis formation.
Precise location of Basel with detailed verbal and map context showing CBD, borders, transport links and river.
Highâquality maps (scale bars, north arrows, legends) support spatial understanding.
Annotated map of BarfÃ¼sserplatz clearly informs transect design using mapping conventions.
Conceptual model succinctly communicates sectoral zoning and theory linkage.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the highest descriptors: the question is firmly grounded in theory, the geographic context is precisely defined and feasible for primary data collection, and mapping conventions are rigorously applied. Hence a full score of 3 is justified.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods (pedestrian counts, landâuse mapping, residential surveys, Spearmanâs rank) are clearly described and linked to hypotheses.
Inclusion of weather and marketâday conditions demonstrates consideration of contextual factors.
Comprehensive landâuse classification key supports systematic data gathering.
weaknesses: Transect table formatting is inconsistent and contains blank cells, reducing clarity.
Justification for sampling parameters (5âminute counts, 100 m radius) is missing.
Theoretical rationale for Spearmanâs rank choice and transect spacing lacks depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Methods are appropriately described and correctly implemented to yield primary data, but explanations lack full detail on sampling design and theoretical justification, aligning with band 2 performance.

strengths: All data collected are directly relevant to the research question and sufficient for substantial analysis.
Presentation techniques (GISâstyle maps, scatter graphs with trend lines, annotated photos) are appropriate and generally effective.
Most figures follow conventions (titles, scales, keys), aiding clarity and interpretation.
weaknesses: Spearman rank table formatting contains inconsistent symbols and typographical errors.
Citation list shows inconsistent formats and incomplete publication details.
Map source citations vary in style across figures.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation uses relevant and sufficient data with appropriate presentation methods and largely correct conventions. Occasional lapses in formatting and citation prevent a higher band, justifying a score of 4.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Robust statistical techniques (Spearmanâs rank) are applied with accurate calculations and clear equations.
Significance testing is wellâexplained, referencing appropriate critical values at 95 % confidence.
Trends and anomalies are thoroughly interpreted and linked to urban models and bidârent theory.
Data interpretation integrates theoretical frameworks effectively, demonstrating advanced analytical skills.
weaknesses: Analysis lacks confidence intervals or more advanced statistical tests for further rigor.
A few typographical errors and referencing omissions (e.g., Figure 17 not immediately cited) interrupt reader flow.
Some minor gaps in linking every pattern back to methods reduce completeness slightly.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The written analysis demonstrates comprehensive statistical application and deep theoretical linkage, exceeding midâband expectations but hampered by small gaps and lack of advanced tests. A score of 6 reflects this highâquality analysis within the 8âmark criterion.

strengths: Conclusion clearly synthesises findings for each hypothesis and explicitly links back to theoretical models.
All statements are fully supported by the presented analysis and evidence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion meets the highest descriptor by providing a clear, fully supported summary of findings, meriting the full 2 marks.

strengths: Evaluation identifies three highly relevant limitations (temporal coverage, site number, observer bias) with comprehensive explanations of their impacts.
Each limitation is paired with detailed, practical improvements (multiâtemporal sampling, expanded sites, automated tools) explaining how validity would increase.
Discussion reflects deep understanding of methodological constraints and enhancement strategies.
weaknesses: Second evaluation point is truncated midâsentence, slightly affecting coherence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation meets topâband descriptors by offering thorough, relevant strengths and weaknesses along with detailed, impactâfocused improvement suggestions, justifying a full score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Figure 1 and subsequent transect maps include scale bars, north arrows, symbols and a key. Spatial information is clear and uncluttered, showing adherence to mapping conventions; therefore the strand reaches band 3.

The research question is explicitly geographical, focused on Baselâs urban morphology, and is firmly grounded in urban land-use theory (Burgess, Hoyt, Multiple Nuclei, bid-rent). Its wording allows hypotheses to be generated and tested. Hence it meets all descriptors of the top band.

Presentation includes GIS-style transect maps, percentage land-use columns, scatter graph with trend line and annotated photographs. These are appropriate and generally effective, though not the absolute optimum for every variable. Band 3-4, score 4, is appropriate.

Data were gathered correctly (21 sites, clear protocols) yet only on one day and in one time-window, limiting reliability. Techniques are appropriate but the volume/replication is modest, fitting âcorrectly used but with minimal dataâ in band 2.

Spearmanâs rank is applied, and descriptive statistics (trend line, anomalies) support interpretation. However, no confidence limits or advanced tests are provided, so analysis is appropriate but not comprehensive, aligning with band 3-4 (score 4).

The student identifies trends and anomalies for each hypothesis and links them to urban models and bid-rent theory. This goes beyond basic description, yet not every pattern is exhaustively tied back to all methods, so mid-high band (5) is fitting.

Basel is precisely located (north-west Switzerland on the Rhine) with a detailed verbal description and a clear city map indicating CBD, borders, transport links and rivers. The context clearly demonstrates feasibility for primary fieldwork along four transects, satisfying the highest descriptor.

Three well-explained limitations (temporal coverage, site number, observer bias) are directly relevant and their effects on results are discussed, meeting the âcomprehensive explanationâ standard of the top band.

The investigation uses relevant pedestrian, land-use and residential quality data for 21 sites, enabling substantial though not exhaustive analysis. Most, though not all, required information is present, so upper mid-band (4) is justified.
The conclusion restates findings for each hypothesis and cites supporting evidence; links to models are made. The support is clear though succinct, fully satisfying the descriptor for band 2.

Analysis addresses all three hypotheses but is weakened by single-visit data and limited statistical testing; some gaps remain in evidential support. This matches âanalysis with some gapsâ, yielding 3 marks.

Methods (pedestrian counts, land-use mapping, residential quality survey, Spearmanâs rank) are described and related to each hypothesis, but explanations lack full detail on sampling intervals, timing schedule and theoretical justification beyond brief statements. This matches band 2.

Figures carry titles, scales and keys, but a few minor convention lapses appear (e.g., inconsistent figure numbering, Greek letters in the table replacing ranks). Occasional errors place it in the 3-4 band; awarding 4 reflects largely correct adherence.

For each limitation, the student proposes detailed, practical improvements (multi-temporal sampling, expanding sites, using automated tools) and explains how these would enhance accuracy. This aligns with the highest band for suggestions.

annotations.comment: The conclusion synthesises findings across models and evidence, delivering a clear, supported summary.
The research question is clearly geographical with explicit links to urban land-use theories, allowing generation and testing of hypotheses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively situates the study within the Geography HL syllabus (Option G, Characteristics of Urban Places), demonstrating strong geographical context.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 1 employs clear mapping conventionsâincluding scale bar, north arrow and legendâsupporting precise locational understanding.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The annotated map of BarfÃ¼sserplatz (Figure 6) follows mapping conventions and clearly shows infrastructure selection for transect design.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The transect table (Figure 7) shows inconsistent formatting and blank cells, making site distributions harder to followâconsider redesigning for clarity.
Including weather and market-day conditions is commendable, as these contextual factors likely influenced pedestrian flows.
The method for pedestrian counting lacks justification for the 5-minute duration and 100 m radiusâexplain why these parameters were chosen.
The land-use classification key is comprehensive, supporting systematic mapping of functional zones.
Provide a rationale for choosing Spearmanâs rank over Pearsonâs correlationâdiscuss non-parametric suitability and data characteristics.
The interpretation links anomalies (Claraplatz spike) to transport corridors and theory, demonstrating strong data-to-theory integration.
The Spearman rank table includes inconsistent symbols (overlines, bold Greek) and formatting errorsâensure full adherence to tabular conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The calculation and presentation of $$r_s$$ in equations is accurate and well-explained, supporting clear statistical analysis.
The significance testing is clearly linked to the critical value (0.370) at 95 % confidence, demonstrating comprehensive statistical interpretation.
The land-use key text contains a typo (âIdustrialâ) and missing separators; refine to improve readability.
Figure 15 is informative, but the text should explicitly reference it when discussing the St. Jakob anomaly for seamless integration.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure 16 mapping of residential quality effectively displays spatial patterns with appropriate symbology and percentage ranges.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The anomaly narrative for Felix-Platter Spital is clear, but referencing Figure 17 immediately after would strengthen reader orientation.
The phrase âsouthest west transectâ contains a typographical errorârevise to âsouthwest transectâ for clarity.
The conceptual model in Figure 19 succinctly communicates the proposed sectoral zoning, linking anomalies to deviations in real urban structure.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The evaluation offers a comprehensive limitation on single-event data collection with a clear improvement plan for multi-temporal sampling.
The second evaluation point is truncated mid-sentenceâcomplete this thought for a fully coherent improvement suggestion.
The works cited list shows inconsistent citation formats and incomplete access detailsâstandardize to a single style and include full publication data.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Subsequent reference lists continue inconsistent formatting; ensure all entries follow the chosen citation standard.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The Burgess Model citation lacks publisher and date detailsâprovide full reference information for academic rigor.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Unify map source citations (e.g., Google Maps, Wikipedia) in style and detail across all figures to avoid inconsistencies.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Provide deeper theoretical rationale for transect spacing and site selection to strengthen the methodâs alignment with urban morphology frameworks.
